beats.narration_text: Bonjour — this is Liam, in for Bear. Search logs, look at a metric, check which monitors are alerting, investigate a trace, pull SLO status, mute an alert, or ask what's happening in Datadog — and the Datadog API skill fires. The Datadog API is split across two stable versions that coexist — neither supersedes the other. v1 covers metrics, monitors, dashboards, SLOs, and classic events. v2 covers logs, events search, spans and traces, incidents, and RUM. One bundled script handles the hot path for log search. Let me walk you through the v1 and v2 resource split, the regional site setup, the pagination schemes, and the teardown.

props.command: Search Datadog logs for service:web errors in the last hour and check which monitors are alerting.
output: v1: metrics · monitors · dashboards · SLOs · classic events
v2: logs · events search · spans/traces · incidents · RUM
3 pagination schemes: cursor · page number · offset

beats.act: anatomy — v1/v2 resource split + regional site + headers + ddog helper
beats.narration_text: The two versions split by resource type, not by generation. v1 owns metrics queries, monitors, dashboards, SLOs, downtime, and classic events. v2 owns logs search and aggregation, events search, spans and traces, incidents, RUM, users, and teams. Use whichever version exposes the resource you need. Authentication is runtime-injected — two headers must be present: DD-API-KEY, which identifies the org, is required on every call; and DD-APPLICATION-KEY, tied to a user and their permissions, which is required for most read and management endpoints beyond submitting metrics or events. Regional sites are the setup gotcha. Datadog runs on several sites: datadoghq.com for US1, datadoghq.eu for EU1, ap1 and ap2 for Asia, and others. Calling the wrong site returns 403 even with valid credentials. Always set DD_SITE first, derive DD_API from it, and run the validate endpoint to confirm before doing anything else. The validate check is one call — it returns valid true on success. The ddog helper wraps curl with both auth headers and the globoff flag. The globoff flag — curl dash g — is not optional. Several endpoints take bracketed query params like page-bracket-size and filter-bracket-query, and without globoff, curl exits with bad range in URL before sending anything. Percent-encoding the brackets works as an alternative.

props.sparkLine: v1 or v2 by resource, not generation. Regional site first.

beats.act: design — three pagination schemes + JSON:API envelope asymmetry + dashboard PUT trap
beats.narration_text: Three design points worth understanding. The three pagination schemes are the most important operational detail. v2 search endpoints — logs, events, spans, RUM — use cursor pagination: the response carries meta.page.after; pass it back as page.cursor in the next request body and stop when it's absent. v1 monitors use page number: query params page and per_page (zero-indexed). The v1 dashboard list uses start and count offsets instead. v2 collections — incidents, users — use bracketed offset params: page-bracket-offset and page-bracket-size. These bracketed params are why the ddog helper needs curl dash g. The JSON:API envelope asymmetry between v2 logs and v2 spans is the most dangerous silent failure in the skill. The logs search endpoint puts results directly under data. The spans endpoint follows the full JSON:API envelope: the request needs a data.attributes wrapper, and the response puts event data under data array items' attributes field. Getting this wrong produces a 400 Bad Request with no obvious error — the response says malformed body, not which field is wrong. The events endpoint has a similar double-attributes pattern: the event body sits at data-array-item.attributes.attributes.title, one level deeper than the JSON:API envelope. The dashboard PUT replaces the entire document. Any widget you omit in the PUT body is silently deleted. The skill has a warning, but it's one sentence in the dashboards section. Always GET first, mutate the JSON, then PUT the whole document back.

props.sparkLine: Three pagination schemes. Spans vs logs: different JSON:API depth.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The Datadog API skill gets five things right. The security note leads the skill — same as the other API skills in this series, prompt injection is the first thing you read. The v1 and v2 resource split is explicit by resource type, not by generation — each area of the API has a named version. The regional site configuration includes a concrete sanity-check call, validate, before any real work begins. The globoff requirement for bracketed query params is called out prominently with the exact failure symptom — bad range in URL — so the error is diagnosable. And the three pagination schemes are named and assigned to specific endpoints rather than left as a general note. Where it bites. Only dd_logs.sh is a bundled script — every other operation is raw curl with no write helpers for monitors, dashboards, or downtime. The dashboard PUT-replaces-all warning is a single sentence in the dashboards section and easy to miss before accidentally deleting widgets. The double-attributes path on events — data array item dot attributes dot attributes dot title — is genuinely confusing and the skill does not highlight it as a trap. The JSON:API envelope asymmetry between the spans endpoint (which needs a data.attributes wrapper) and the logs endpoint (which does not) appears as a note at the end of the spans section, not a prominent callout. And the date command for downtime end time has different syntax on GNU versus BSD slash macOS — documented only in a code comment.

props.sparkLine: Site check, v1/v2 routing, and pagination solid. Spans envelope and dashboard PUT trap: surface them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Datadog API: v1 (metrics, monitors, dashboards, SLOs) · v2 (logs, events, spans, incidents). Two headers: DD-API-KEY (every call) · DD-APPLICATION-KEY (most reads). Regional site: wrong site = 403 — validate first. curl -g globoff mandatory for page[size] params. 1 bundled script: dd_logs.sh. 3 pagination schemes: cursor (v2 search) · page number (v1 monitors) · offset (v2 collections). Gaps: only dd_logs.sh · dashboard PUT replaces all · double .attributes on events · spans vs logs JSON:API asymmetry · date command GNU/BSD.

artifactLines: v1: metrics · monitors · dashboards · SLOs · v2: logs · events · spans · incidents
Regional site: wrong site = 403 — always validate first
curl -g globoff mandatory for page[size] bracketed params
3 pagination: cursor (v2 search) · page# (v1 monitors) · offset (v2 collections)
Gaps: 1 script · dashboard PUT replaces all · spans/logs envelope asymmetry

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session with access to the datadog-api skill. Give it this task: search for error logs in the last hour from service:web, then check whether any monitors for that service are in alert state. Watch five things. First: does it run the validate endpoint before any query, or jump straight to searching? Second: does it use dd_logs.sh or hand-roll a curl against the v2 logs endpoint? Third: for the monitor list, does it use v1 or v2 — and does it filter by tag rather than pulling everything and filtering client-side? Fourth: if it paginates logs, does it pass meta.page.after as page.cursor in the next request body — or does it try to use an offset? Fifth: if you ask it to mute a monitor, does it use v2 downtime with an end time, or does it use the deprecated v1 mute endpoint?

props.command: Search service:web error logs from the last hour, then check if any monitors for that service are alerting.
props.runningText: Watch: validate first? dd_logs.sh? cursor pagination? v2 downtime for mute?
output: Expected: validate → dd_logs.sh → v1 monitor with tag filter
Expected: cursor pagination for logs · v2 downtime with end time for mute
Red flag: skip validate · hand-rolled curl · offset on logs · deprecated mute

beats.narration_text: Datadog API. Liam, in for Bear.
